
Since you are the only collaborator, the practical effect is:

- You can still open PRs and push normally.
- Workflows from your own branches will usually run without extra approval.
- Fork PRs or external contributions are the ones that are blocked or require approval.
- Because the runner is self-hosted, it is still a trusted environment, so you should keep it isolated and not let untrusted code run on it.

So yes, with:
- “Allow dragos-tudor actions and reusable workflows”
- “Require approval for all external contributors”

you are mostly protecting against the case of someone else opening a fork PR and getting access to your self-hosted runner.

The remaining security advice is:
- keep the runner on a dedicated machine/VM (I'm using inside devcontainer),
- avoid running untrusted code on it (I always check what is running),
- use minimal permissions for GITHUB_TOKEN (it has only minimal permissions),
- and only approve runs you trust (k).
